_note: Taking-money checkpoints. Cross-domain — ecommerce, SaaS subscriptions, marketplaces, and booking all reuse these.

checkpoints.title: Idempotent payment handling
checkpoints.why: Retries, double-clicks, and webhook re-delivery cause double charges or duplicate orders or actions without idempotency.

levels.3: No idempotency key; duplicate submissions can double-charge.
levels.6: Idempotency key on payment creation AND the webhook handler de-duplicates by provider event id before mutating state; both retried creations and re-delivered webhooks are processed exactly once.
levels.9: Idempotency enforced structurally across the full path — a unique constraint or idempotency store makes duplicate processing impossible — with safe replay and reconciliation of duplicate deliveries.

verify.reasoning: Can the same payment be created twice from a retry? Are provider webhooks processed exactly once? Is there an idempotency key on creation and webhook handling?

assertions.check: Idempotency key passed to payment provider on intent creation.
Webhook handler de-duplicates by provider event id before mutating state.

checkpoints.title: Payment state cannot diverge from your records
checkpoints.why: Your record of a payment and the provider's actual payment status drifting apart (paid-no-record, record-unpaid) is a top source of silent revenue loss.

levels.3: The record is marked paid optimistically on client return, before provider confirmation.
levels.6: Record transitions are driven by server-side provider webhooks (webhook authenticity is graded separately by payment.webhook_authenticity), not by client redirects.
levels.9: Explicit state machine, reconciliation job catching divergence, per-record audit trail.

verify.reasoning: Is paid set from a server-side webhook signal or from the client redirect? Can a record be marked paid without a server-side confirmation, or money captured without a record? (Whether that webhook's signature is authenticated is payment.webhook_authenticity's concern, not this one.)

assertions.check: Paid transition is set from a provider webhook, not a client success/redirect route.
A reconciliation job/test detects and repairs divergence between provider and local state.

checkpoints.title: Card data never touches your server; payment secrets stay server-side
checkpoints.why: Receiving or storing raw card numbers drags the whole app into PCI-DSS scope and is the most expensive class of mistake when taking payments. The safe path - provider-hosted fields / tokenization - is well documented, yet AI-generated payment flows often POST the PAN to a first-party backend. Equally fatal: the provider SECRET key shipped in the client bundle or committed to the repo.

levels.3: Raw card fields submitted to your own backend, or secret API keys present in client-side code / committed config.
levels.6: Card data captured by the provider's hosted fields/SDK so the PAN never reaches your server; only a token is handled; secret keys are server-side only.
levels.9: Tokenization end to end, secrets injected from a managed store (never in repo or bundle), and the integration documents its PCI-DSS scope (e.g. SAQ A) and holds to it.

verify.reasoning: Does any raw card number (PAN, CVV) reach first-party code, or is it captured directly by the provider (hosted fields / Elements / SDK) and exchanged for a token? Is the provider secret key referenced only server-side, never in client bundles or committed files?

assertions.check: No endpoint or form submits raw PAN/CVV to first-party code; payment uses a provider token.
Provider secret keys are read from a server-side env/secret store, not present in client bundles or committed config.

checkpoints.title: Payment webhooks are authenticated before they are trusted
checkpoints.why: Payment state is driven by provider webhooks (see payment.state_integrity). If the webhook endpoint does not verify the provider's signature, anyone who discovers the URL can POST a forged 'payment succeeded' and mark records paid for free. Signature verification is documented by every major provider and routinely omitted in AI-generated handlers.

levels.3: Webhook endpoint trusts the request body with no signature or authenticity check.
levels.6: Provider signature verified against the signing secret and the raw (unparsed) request body before any state change; invalid or unsigned requests rejected.
levels.9: Signature plus timestamp/replay-window verified, the signing secret sourced from a managed store, and verification failures logged/alerted.

verify.reasoning: Does the webhook handler verify the provider's signature against the raw request body before mutating any payment/record state? Are unsigned or invalid-signature requests rejected? Is the signing secret kept server-side?

assertions.check: Webhook handler verifies the provider signature before processing the event.
A test sends an unsigned/invalid-signature webhook and the handler rejects it without changing state.

checkpoints.title: Refunds and cancellations are correct, bounded, and idempotent
checkpoints.why: The reverse-money path is built late and tested least. Without guards you can refund more than was captured, double-refund on retry or webhook redelivery, or refund without reversing related state - each a direct cash or inventory loss.

levels.3: Refunds issued ad hoc with no cap against the captured amount and no dedup; retries can refund twice.
levels.6: Refund amount bounded by the remaining captured balance, idempotent against retries/webhook redelivery, and related state adjusted.
levels.9: Full refund/cancel state machine: partial refunds tracked cumulatively, idempotent end to end, related state reconciled, audit trail per action.

verify.reasoning: Can a refund exceed what was captured, or sum past it across partial refunds? Is a retried or re-delivered refund request de-duplicated? Does a refund or cancellation reverse the related state (inventory, entitlement)?

assertions.check: Refund amount is validated against the remaining captured/refundable balance.
Refund operations are idempotent (keyed so a retry does not double-refund).
